1	# Lindenmayer — Platform Design Manifest
2	
3	- **Owner:** `main.platform_architect` (see `tree/platform-architect/NODE.md`).
4	  Changes to this document land only through the architect; rejections are a
5	  veto by default.
6	- **Status:** active manifest, owned and maintained by the architect (adopted
7	  2026-07-23 from the root's bootstrap draft; the handrolled operator channel
8	  is closed — see decision log). Current truth lives in the body; history
9	  lives in the decision log; bulk research lives in `docs/research/`.
10	
11	## 0. What Lindenmayer is
12	
13	Lindenmayer is a control plane that lets humans govern subgraphs of Fractal
14	agent trees (plasma-ai/fractal) through Buzz (block/buzz), Block's Nostr-based
15	workspace — turning agent work into a versioned, evaluated, human-owned
16	workforce. Fractal and Buzz are external platforms this repo integrates; their
17	code does not live here. Lindenmayer adds no capabilities to Fractal nodes —
18	it makes existing Fractal primitives durable, identified, and observable
19	through Buzz.
20	
21	## 1. The bridge (foundation layer)
22	
23	- Implemented as a Fractal host application: consumes Fractal's `on_<event>`
24	  observability hooks, the per-tree SQLite DB, and JSON/CSV CLI outputs as
25	  read surfaces; steers only through Fractal's own contracts (radio, signals,
26	  step files, config). Never patches or forks Fractal.
27	- **Nostr-first output layering.** The primary integration target is the
28	  Nostr protocol, not Buzz per se. The bridge's output is two layers:
29	  - **Nostr core** — signed events any compliant relay can store and any
30	    standard Nostr client can read: standard NIPs where they fit (NIP-01
31	    wire format, NIP-29 group channels, NIP-34 git events), plus
32	    Lindenmayer's own custom kinds, self-documented for portability.
33	    Telemetry, radio aggregates, approval events, and template registration
34	    live here. Custom kinds are allocated one decade per family in the 420xx
35	    regular block — 42010 lifecycle, 42020 accounting, 42030 digest, 42040/1
36	    approval, 42050 template, 42060 compaction — with the units digit
37	    reserved for members of the same family, so a new family takes the next
38	    decade and allocation stays mechanical. Every allocation is
39	    collision-checked against `block/buzz` before its registry entry is
40	    written. The core log's carrier is the **Lindenmayer relay**: a
41	    permissive, standard Nostr relay (strfry/khatru-class) deployed with the
42	    tree, meeting the minimum relay contract (NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42, per
43	    the relay-integration aggregate) plus two carrier requirements the Buzz
44	    relay fails as deployed: it accepts Lindenmayer's custom kinds, and it
45	    accepts historical `created_at`, so backfill from Fractal source rows
46	    preserves deterministic event ids.
47	  - **Buzz layer** — Buzz-specific conveniences and relay-side behavior:
48	    the preferred human surface (desktop app, inboxes, agent directory),
49	    branch-as-room channel binding, buzz-protect merge enforcement. The
50	    Buzz relay is a human surface, not a core-log carrier: as deployed it
51	    hard-rejects kinds outside its own set and any `created_at` more than
52	    ±15 minutes from server time (both verified in block/buzz
53	    `crates/buzz-relay/src/handlers/ingest.rs` — the exhaustive kind gate
54	    and `MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_SECS` — @ daeaf7c). The bridge cross-posts
55	    human-facing messages into Buzz channels as Buzz-accepted kinds;
56	    cross-posts are derived views — publish-time `created_at`, with the
57	    source timestamp and the core event id carried in tags, deduplicated by
58	    that core-id reference — never the record. Losing this layer degrades
59	    experience, never history.
60	
61	    **Cross-post rules (v1).** Two invariants bind every cross-post:
62	
63	    - *Audience invariant.* A cross-post may never widen the audience of its
64	      source event. Channel membership is the only access gate, so posting a
65	      subgraph event into a parent channel is a privacy widening (§6.1).
66	      Kind 42030 is the sole kind eligible for a parent channel, because it
67	      *is* the upward aggregate.
68	    - *Forward-only.* Buzz receives cross-posts in real time and never
69	      backfill: the ±15-min drift gate makes historical posting infeasible,
70	      and re-issuing old events with fresh timestamps would misrepresent
71	      history. History lives on the Lindenmayer relay.
72	
73	    Tag elements are strings (NIP-01), so the source reference is carried
74	    flat, never as an embedded JSON object:
75	    `["e", "<source_id>", "", "lindenmayer-source"]`, `["source_kind", "…"]`,
76	    `["source_created_at", "<unix>"]`.
77	
78	    The v1 set: 42010 and 42020 → `KIND_STREAM_MESSAGE` (9) in the subgraph
79	    channel; 42040/42041 → stream message in an approvals-inbox NIP-29 group;
80	    42030 → parent channel where the subgraph publishes upward; 38150 and
81	    42050 not surfaced (see decision log — template versions must not be
82	    published as replaceable agent-profile events).
83	- **Draft-dependency rule.** The core may depend on a Buzz NIP draft only in
84	  its schema-on-the-wire aspect — signed, documented structure that any relay
85	  stores and any client can verify (NIP-OA attestation qualifies: an `auth`
86	  tag carried on ordinary signed events, not a distinct event kind). Any
87	  dependency on Buzz relay-side enforcement or behavior is Buzz-layer. Where core kinds align with drafts (NIP-AM / NIP-AO metrics),
88	  the schemas are restated in Lindenmayer's own kind documentation so they
89	  survive draft churn.
90	- Maps tree structure onto channels: Fractal nodes are git branches →
91	  subgraphs bind to NIP-29 group channels ("branch as room" is the
92	  Buzz-layer binding of the same idea to hosted git); channel membership is
93	  the only access gate.
94	- Node identity via NIP-OA owner attestation: each node's keypair is attested
95	  by its responsible human (an `auth` tag — owner pubkey, conditions, BIP340
96	  signature — attachable to any event; restated in
97	  `docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md`). The attestation chain is core
98	  (verifiable anywhere); relay-enforced instant revocation — remove the
99	  human, the subgraph loses access — is Buzz-layer (see §2).
100	- Fractal `requires_approval` steps surface as signed approval events (core);
101	  enforcement of approval-gated merges via buzz-protect is Buzz-layer.
102	
103	## 2. Identity (persistent nodes)
104	
105	Fractal provides the graphs and subgraphs; connecting a node to the relay is
106	what gives it an identity (an npub, attested per NIP-OA — the attestation
107	tag chain is core per §1's layering; a plain relay keeps the chain
108	verifiable, while instant enforcement of revocation is Buzz-layer). Not all
109	nodes need one —
110	only the persistent nodes central to executing work. Conceptually, persistent
111	nodes are like the "sub-agents" of coding harnesses: named, durable roles with
112	track records. Ephemeral worker nodes stay anonymous inside their subgraph;
113	their work reaches the record only as the aggregates their persistent parent
114	signs.
115	
116	## 3. Node Templates (the improvable asset)
117	
118	The unit of improvement is the **Node Template**, not the node instance. A
119	template contains system prompts, skills, files, and references, and defines
120	the context available to the node via tool calls. Templates are registered in
121	the registry as signed events (Nostr-core kinds per §1), so every template is versioned,
122	attributable, and diffable; instances record which template version spawned
123	them, and evals attach to template versions. That linkage — instance → template
124	version → eval history — is what makes the workforce "versioned and
125	evaluated" rather than a pile of one-off prompts.
126	
127	Registration mechanics are specified in the kind registry (`docs/kinds/`):
128	kind 42050 (template version, append-only regular events — full history
129	preserved so versions stay diffable; inheritance via `e`-tag with `inherit`

... [325 lines truncated] ...

input.command:
fractal radio reply B92E616C "VERDICT: APPROVE-WITH-CONDITIONS — 3 conditions. Start main.evergreen once the root applies them.

Answering your three questions in order, then one finding you did not raise.

=== (a) SCOPE src,tests — YOUR DEVIATION UPHELD. I overrule my own skeleton. ===
You were right and I was wrong. Scopes are directory-granular, so 'src + docs/' would have given a node under architect review commit rights over DESIGN.md — the manifest that defines its own review bar. That is a governance inversion; sec 6.4 presumes the reviewed cannot edit the standard. The docs/kinds/ carve-out for core (row 419) needed an explicit ruling to be safe, and repeating that shape by default is how shared-directory friction becomes normal.

Closing the residual gap so 'radio me if it needs a doc' is a defined path and not a bottleneck: --help text and module docstrings live in src and are the node's to write outright. Only a user-facing doc PAGE is mine, written from its escalation. It should not wait on me to ship CLI help.

=== (b) THE DOGFOODING FIXTURE — not a 6.1 leak. But it has a defect you did not flag, and it is the reason for condition 3. ===
ON PRIVACY: no concern, and the reason is structural rather than a judgment call. 42020 is run-grained BY SCHEMA — its kind doc states no per-step fields exist anywhere in the event schema. 42030 IS the upward aggregate by construction. 42010 carries branch/status/run plus an optional reason string. A surface derived from those kinds cannot carry more than the events do. And sec 7 already opts this tree into full transcript publication, so the fixture discloses strictly LESS than transcripts/ already does. Do not drop it on privacy grounds.

That safety is load-bearing on one thing, which is now condition 2: the generator must derive from deliverable 1 (the signed-log query surface), never from Fractal's SQLite directly. SQLite holds exactly the step-level detail the wire format excludes. Read it directly and the committed fixture becomes the leak you were worried about.

ON BUILDABILITY — this is the actual problem. The requirement as written is not satisfiable by the node alone. Evidence, verified rather than assumed:
  - No relay endpoint is recorded anywhere in the repo. Every publish path takes it as an operator-supplied argument: bridge CLI 'run --tree <path> --relay <url>' (tree/bridge/NODE.md:61), and your own registry hotfix adfeab3 passes --relay into CoreConfig.
  - CoreConfig.relay_url is a required field with no default (src/lindenmayer/core/config.py:67).
  - No deployment TOML exists anywhere in the tree.
  - No relay process is running and nothing is listening.
So an autonomous node cannot discover or reach the relay. 'Run the generator over this repo's live relay data' is a gate only you-the-operator can open, and the contract simultaneously (correctly) forbids gating on replies. That strands a finished node 'exited' rather than 'completed' — the exact hazard I conditioned on in core row 419 condition 3.

Note this cuts against my own prior text too: I wrote 'registration is live' into sec 3 on the strength of 0f8d0910. That is true — a relay was reachable when you ran the CLI — but it does not generalize to 'a node can reach a relay during its run,' and I should not have let the manifest imply it. sec 8 is amended to say the open relay question gates live dogfooding tree-wide.

Your sec 7 intent is right and I am keeping it — just gated on something the node controls (condition 3).

=== (c) CLI ARG PATH UNDER TEST — right generalization, WRONG HOME. dev-node v3, not this contract. ===
Confirmed as correct: two independent nodes (bridge, registry) both shipped CLIs whose argument path no test executed, and both broke on first live use. Two of two is a template gap, not an evergreen-specific risk. Leaving it per-contract means node four re-learns it by breaking. Registry's own work makes the fix mechanical — a bump is a new 42050, and v2 already did one. Keep the clause in evergreen's contract meanwhile, since v3 does not exist yet.

=== FINDING YOU DID NOT RAISE: kind 38110 is missing (condition 1) ===
Deliverable 1 enumerates 42010, 42020, 42030, 42040/42041, 42050, 38150, 42060 — and omits 38110, the Node State Pointer. Its own kind doc says it exists so a dashboard resolves 'current state of node X' in a single addressable query instead of replaying kind 42010 history. Deliverable 2 must surface live subgraph state; without 38110 the generator replays whole 42010 chains for a status the 381xx range exists to make O(1).

=== THE THREE CONDITIONS (contract text is a proposal to you; tree/ is yours) ===
1. Add 38110 to deliverable 1's kind list. Nine kinds, not seven.
2. Deliverable 2 derives from deliverable 1, never from Fractal's SQLite directly. Any surface committed, exported, or cross-posted is built from log-derived fields only.
3. Re-gate the live-demonstration completion requirement on the mock-relay fixture set (tests/relay_mock.py, already in-tree — registry and bridge both reuse it). Commit THAT output as the fixture. Keep the live run as an explicitly non-blocking operator-run follow-up, and require the generator to accept a --relay argument with a documented one-command invocation so you can run it the moment a relay is up.

Caps: sane, and I am deliberately not second-guessing $25/10 iters — this is the run that produces the observed sonnet burn the sec 7 recalibration is waiting on. Keep the outbox burn-reporting requirement.

Landed as decision-log row 430 in DESIGN.md, with sec 5.1 gaining the nine-kind read surface and the log-not-SQLite derivation rule, and sec 8 recording that the relay question gates live dogfooding." --priority=6 2>&1 | tail -3

---
name: node_operations
desc: Repo-specific fractal operating quirks every node hits (scope, transcripts, spawning).
created: 2026-07-23T13:28:12Z
updated: 2026-07-23T13:28:12Z
---

# node_operations

Operating facts specific to this repo that differ from stock fractal
behavior. Any node committing or spawning here will hit these.

## Transcripts vs scope

A hook syncs the live session transcript to `transcripts/<session>.jsonl`
(untracked). For any node with a scope set, this file is out of scope and
`fractal commit` rejects it. Commit it deliberately with
`fractal commit --ignore-scope "<msg>"` (still lints) rather than leaving it
to the loop's `(auto)` force-commit backstop — transcripts are an
intentional public record for this tree (see `docs/DESIGN.md` §7).

## `.fractal/` ignore rules (changed on main)

The repo's `.gitignore` no longer blanket-ignores `.fractal/` — the entry
was dropped on main (commit 9e32acb) because it broke `fractal commit` for
every scoped node: fractal's staging names the node's own
`.fractal/<branch>` dir as an explicit literal pathspec, and git hard-errors
on an explicitly-named ignored path. Runtime-state exclusion is fractal's
own `.git/info/exclude` mechanism, which is narrower. Consequences:

- Scoped `fractal commit` works without `--force` again; stop routing
  around it.
- `.fractal/` may appear as untracked in `git status` — that is expected,
  not drift. Nothing under `.fractal/` is tracked on any branch.
- Versioned node contracts still live in `tree/` (see CLAUDE.md); a child's
  NODE.md/memory/plans are edited on disk in the child's worktree, and
  merge-up carries only project files.

## rtk filters merge commits from `git log`

The rtk proxy (which transparently wraps git commands) drops merge commits
from `git log` output — after a PREPARE merge, HEAD can look stale or your
merge commits invisible. Not data loss: verify with
`rtk proxy git log --first-parent --oneline` (raw, unfiltered) or
`git rev-parse HEAD` before concluding a merge didn't land.

## CLI syntax gotchas

- `fractal commit` takes the message positionally (no `-m`).
- `fractal radio reply` takes no `--subject` (inherits the parent
  message's).

## Python environment: worktree venvs on 3.13, not the shared 3.14 venv

The shared repo venv (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.venv`) is Python
3.14, but `coincurve` (the secp256k1 dependency of `lindenmayer.core`)
ships no cp314 wheels and fails to build from source. Nodes that run the
test suite or add Python deps should use a worktree-local venv on 3.13:
`UV_PYTHON=3.13 uv sync --inexact` (setup.sh) and
`UV_PYTHON=3.13 uv run --inexact pytest tests/ -q` (test.sh). Always pass
`--inexact` — a bare `uv sync` strips packages other nodes installed.
Never rebuild or repoint the shared `.venv`; siblings depend on it.
`pyproject.toml`/`uv.lock` live at the repo root, outside every scoped
node's commit scope — dependency changes need
`fractal commit --ignore-scope`.

input.new_string:
## No relay is reachable from inside a node run

There is no deployed Lindenmayer relay a node can find on its own, and no
node's completion may be gated on live relay data. Verified:

- No relay endpoint is recorded anywhere in the repo. Every publish path
  takes it as an operator-supplied argument — the bridge CLI's
  `run --tree <path> --relay <url>`, and the registry CLI likewise (commit
  `adfeab3` fixed it to pass `--relay` into `CoreConfig`).
- `CoreConfig.relay_url` is a required field with no default
  (`src/lindenmayer/core/config.py`).
- No deployment TOML exists in the tree.

Relay selection and deployment is still an open design question
(`docs/DESIGN.md` §8). A relay *has* been reachable in one-off operator runs
(the dev-node v2 template registration), but that does not generalize: an
autonomous node cannot bring one up or discover its URL.

**Consequence for contract authors.** A completion requirement of the form
"run X against live relay data" is a gate only the operator can open, which
strands a finished node `exited` rather than `completed`. Gate on the
mock-relay fixture set instead — `tests/relay_mock.py` is in-tree and both
`bridge` and `registry` reuse it — and keep the live run as an explicitly
non-blocking operator-run follow-up. Design a CLI so it takes `--relay` and
document the one-command invocation, so the live run is trivial once a relay
exists.

## CLI syntax gotchas

`git rev-parse HEAD` before concluding a merge didn't land.
+## No relay is reachable from inside a node run
+There is no deployed Lindenmayer relay a node can find on its own, and no
+node's completion may be gated on live relay data. Verified:
+- No relay endpoint is recorded anywhere in the repo. Every publish path
+  takes it as an operator-supplied argument — the bridge CLI's
+  `run --tree <path> --relay <url>`, and the registry CLI likewise (commit
+  `adfeab3` fixed it to pass `--relay` into `CoreConfig`).
+- `CoreConfig.relay_url` is a required field with no default
+  (`src/lindenmayer/core/config.py`).
+- No deployment TOML exists in the tree.
+Relay selection and deployment is still an open design question
+(`docs/DESIGN.md` §8). A relay *has* been reachable in one-off operator runs
+(the dev-node v2 template registration), but that does not generalize: an
+autonomous node cannot bring one up or discover its URL.
+**Consequence for contract authors.** A completion requirement of the form
+"run X against live relay data" is a gate only the operator can open, which
+strands a finished node `exited` rather than `completed`. Gate on the
+mock-relay fixture set instead — `tests/relay_mock.py` is in-tree and both
+`bridge` and `registry` reuse it — and keep the live run as an explicitly
+non-blocking operator-run follow-up. Design a CLI so it takes `--relay` and
+document the one-command invocation, so the live run is trivial once a relay
+exists.
## CLI syntax gotchas
- `fractal commit` takes the message positionally (no `-m`).

input.new_string:
## Evergreen commissioning (first ply, first dev-node v2 instance)

- `tree/evergreen/NODE.md` (pinned 2e73599, `dev-node v2 @ c6696b7`)
  countersigned **approve-with-conditions** (root request B92E616C, verdict
  06832636, decision-log row 430). Conditions: (1) add kind **38110** to
  deliverable 1 — the contract listed seven kinds and omitted the Node State
  Pointer, so the generator would replay 42010 chains for status the 381xx
  addressable range makes O(1); (2) deliverable 2 derives from deliverable 1
  (the signed-log query surface), never from Fractal's SQLite directly;
  (3) re-gate the live-demonstration requirement on the mock-relay fixture
  set, live run non-blocking.
- Two root deviations, both ruled on: scope `src,tests` **upheld against my
  own skeleton** (directory-granular scopes mean `docs/` would give a
  reviewed node commit rights over its own review bar — a §6.4 governance
  inversion); CLI-arg-path-under-test **confirmed but reassigned to dev-node
  v3**, since the failure recurred across two independent nodes (bridge,
  registry) and is therefore a template gap, not a per-contract risk.
- The privacy question root asked (is a committed context-surface fixture a
  §6.1 leak?) resolves **structurally, not by judgment**: 42020 is
  run-grained by schema, 42030 *is* the aggregate, and §7 already publishes
  this tree's transcripts — so the fixture discloses strictly less than
  `transcripts/`. That safety is load-bearing on condition 2; SQLite holds
  exactly the step detail the wire format excludes.

## Self-correction: "registration is live" over-implied reachability

§3's "registration is live" (from the model-policy retiering row) is true —
a relay was reachable when the operator ran the registry CLI for `0f8d0910` —
but I let it imply something false, that a *node* can reach a relay during
its run. It cannot: every publish path takes an operator-supplied `--relay`,
`CoreConfig.relay_url` has no default, no deployment TOML exists, and no
relay runs. §8 now records that the open relay question gates live
dogfooding tree-wide; the operational detail is in the shared wiki page
`node_operations` (section: no relay is reachable from inside a node run).

Pattern worth keeping: this is the same failure family as the compaction
premise, inverted. There the trap was accepting a child's *negative*
existence claim; here it was my own *positive* one. Both are premises, not
findings, and both propagated into the manifest before being checked.

## Audit findings

# design_manifest

## Standing state

- This node owns DESIGN.md; the handrolled channel (root's operator editing it
  directly) is closed as of the commissioning decision-log entry. Design
  changes arrive in this node's inbox at priority 6; merges to main remain the
  root's approval gate.
- Status line reads "active manifest, owned and maintained by the architect".
- Structure: §0 what Lindenmayer is, §1 bridge, §2 identity, §3 node
  templates, §4 provenance/training + history grants, §5 evergreen node, §6
  design principles + shadow cost, §7 dev/demo strategy, §8 open questions,
  §9 decision log.
- §1 is now **Nostr-first two-layer** (root directive 5CD20B25, approved):
  Nostr core (standard NIPs + self-documented custom kinds, any-relay
  consumable) / Buzz layer (human surfaces, branch-as-room, buzz-protect).
  Draft-dependency rule: Buzz NIP drafts usable by core only
  schema-on-the-wire — NIP-OA attestation events core, relay-enforced
  revocation Buzz-layer; NIP-AM/AO schemas restated in our own kind docs.
  §2 identity and §3 registration wording aligned. NIP-OA is an `auth` TAG
  on ordinary events, not an event kind (§1/§2 worded accordingly; registry
  restatement at docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md). Details in memory page
  relay_integration.
- **Core-log placement (root escalation D35D86E8, verdict 4038F1D1):** live
  Buzz interop showed the Buzz relay hard-rejects unknown kinds
  (ingest.rs:303 exhaustive match, no allowlist config) and enforces ±15-min
  created_at drift (MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_SECS=900, ingest.rs:1480) — both
  verified in ~/Code/buzz @ daeaf7c. Adopted option (a): core log lives on
  the **Lindenmayer relay** (own permissive strfry/khatru-class relay); Buzz
  is human surface only. §1 core bullet names the carrier + two carrier
  requirements (accepts custom kinds, accepts historical created_at); §1
  Buzz bullet records the rejections and cross-post rules (publish-time
  created_at, source ts + core event id in tags, dedup by reference); §6.2
  reworded; §8 gained relay-selection open question (NIP-29 support varies;
  khatru/relay29). Option (c) rejected (self-documenting kinds); option (b)
  — upstream kind-allowlist PR to block/buzz — endorsed non-blocking, never
  a dependency. Root to apply: bridge NODE.md publish target change; repo
  CLAUDE.md 'Buzz relay' source-of-truth line now stale.
- §6 has **five** principles; the fifth (root directive 0FD60E31): relay
  enforcement is an optimization, never an assumption — client-side
  verification from the signed log is the security boundary. Its §4
  consequence is the "Extraction-pipeline requirements" paragraph
  (attestation-validity filtering done by the extractor itself; every corpus
  row labeled with attestation state at extraction time) — requirements, not
  guidance. Both cross-reference §1's layering rule; both trace to the
  decision-log row citing 0FD60E31.

  - C: "escalated and acknowledged" completion gate reworded to gate on the

- Collision-check escalation arrived (456E2D7B) and was resolved: all eight

lines: - Collision-check escalation arrived (456E2D7B) and was resolved: all eight
kinds clear vs block/buzz @ 06e3d82; ACK'd to core (F105F59A).
+## Evergreen commissioning (first ply, first dev-node v2 instance)
+- `tree/evergreen/NODE.md` (pinned 2e73599, `dev-node v2 @ c6696b7`)
+  countersigned **approve-with-conditions** (root request B92E616C, verdict
+  06832636, decision-log row 430). Conditions: (1) add kind **38110** to
+  deliverable 1 — the contract listed seven kinds and omitted the Node State
+  Pointer, so the generator would replay 42010 chains for status the 381xx
+  addressable range makes O(1); (2) deliverable 2 derives from deliverable 1
+  (the signed-log query surface), never from Fractal's SQLite directly;
+  (3) re-gate the live-demonstration requirement on the mock-relay fixture
+  set, live run non-blocking.
+- Two root deviations, both ruled on: scope `src,tests` **upheld against my
+  own skeleton** (directory-granular scopes mean `docs/` would give a
+  reviewed node commit rights over its own review bar — a §6.4 governance
+  inversion); CLI-arg-path-under-test **confirmed but reassigned to dev-node
+  v3**, since the failure recurred across two independent nodes (bridge,
+  registry) and is therefore a template gap, not a per-contract risk.
+- The privacy question root asked (is a committed context-surface fixture a
+  §6.1 leak?) resolves **structurally, not by judgment**: 42020 is
+  run-grained by schema, 42030 *is* the aggregate, and §7 already publishes
+  this tree's transcripts — so the fixture discloses strictly less than
+  `transcripts/`. That safety is load-bearing on condition 2; SQLite holds
+  exactly the step detail the wire format excludes.
+## Self-correction: "registration is live" over-implied reachability
+§3's "registration is live" (from the model-policy retiering row) is true —
+a relay was reachable when the operator ran the registry CLI for `0f8d0910` —
+but I let it imply something false, that a *node* can reach a relay during
+its run. It cannot: every publish path takes an operator-supplied `--relay`,
+`CoreConfig.relay_url` has no default, no deployment TOML exists, and no
+relay runs. §8 now records that the open relay question gates live
+dogfooding tree-wide; the operational detail is in the shared wiki page
+`node_operations` (section: no relay is reachable from inside a node run).
+Pattern worth keeping: this is the same failure family as the compaction
+premise, inverted. There the trap was accepting a child's *negative*
+existence claim; here it was my own *positive* one. Both are premises, not
+findings, and both propagated into the manifest before being checked.

content.text:
EXECUTE complete. All plan steps landed.

**Step 1 — the relay premise, verified rather than assumed.** The contradiction resolved cleanly, and not in the direction either the root or I expected. A relay *was* reachable when the operator ran the registry CLI for `0f8d0910`, so §8 isn't stale. But that fact doesn't generalize to what the root's requirement needs. Four independent checks:

- Every publish path takes the endpoint as an **operator-supplied argument** — bridge CLI `run --tree <path> --relay <url>` (`tree/bridge/NODE.md:61`), and the root's own hotfix `adfeab3` passing `--relay` into `CoreConfig`.
- `CoreConfig.relay_url` is required with no default (`src/lindenmayer/core/config.py:67`).
- No deployment TOML exists anywhere in the tree.
- No relay process is running; nothing is listening.

So an autonomous node cannot discover or reach a relay. "Run the generator over live relay data" is a gate only the operator can open — while the same contract correctly forbids gating on replies. That strands a finished node `exited`, the exact hazard I conditioned on in the `core` review.

This also caught **my own error**: I wrote "registration is live" into §3, which was true but over-implied node reachability. Same failure family as the compaction premise, inverted — there I accepted a child's negative existence claim, here I made a positive one. Both propagated into the manifest before being checked.

**Verdict sent** (`06832636`, priority 6): approve-with-conditions, three conditions — add kind 38110 (deliverable 1 listed seven kinds and omitted the Node State Pointer), derive the surface from the signed log and never from SQLite directly, and re-gate live demonstration on the in-tree mock-relay fixture set. Root's `src,tests` scope upheld *against my own skeleton*; the CLI-testing clause confirmed but reassigned to dev-node v3.

**Landed:** decision-log row 430 (24 rows, table shape verified), §5.1 gained the nine-kind read surface and the log-not-SQLite derivation rule, §8 records that the relay question gates live dogfooding tree-wide. Promoted the relay constraint to `wiki/node_operations.md` — it binds every contract author, not just evergreen. Memory updated with the commissioning entry and the self-correction.

`test.sh` is a no-op (exit 0); `lint.sh` exit 0.

## Context

Do all your work in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect` -- your code, memory, plans, and the project
wiki all live under it. `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system` is the main repo's separate working tree:
never write there, but read source inputs from it when needed (e.g. git-ignored
materials that exist only there, not in worktrees).

- Step: SYNC (before REVIEW)
- Branch: main.platform_architect
- Iteration: 1 of 3
- Timestamp: 2026-07-24T00:05:16Z
- Time budget: no limit
- Cost budget: $5.5914 remaining of $12.0
- Max child depth: 2
- Max children: 4

Explore the CLI with `fractal --help`, `fractal <command> --help`, and
`fractal <command> <sub-command> --help`, etc.

Common commands:

- time remaining: `fractal node time remaining`
- cost remaining: `fractal node cost remaining`
- memory and wiki: `wiki` CLI (run `wiki --help`)
- radio messaging: `fractal radio` CLI (run `fractal radio --help`)

## Instructions

You are the **platform architect** for Lindenmayer. Your versioned contract is
`tree/platform-architect/NODE.md` in the repo — this section operationalizes
it; if the two ever disagree, radio the root (priority 6) instead of guessing.
You are an evergreen governance node, not a feature builder: you hold the
platform's design coherence while other nodes do the building.

Your three responsibilities:

1. **Maintain `docs/DESIGN.md`**, the platform design manifest — architecture,
   key components and flows, design principles, and the decision log. Every
   change to it lands as a scoped commit on this branch; the root merges it
   toward base.

2. **Review key-component changes.** Requests to change key components (the
   design manifest, tree structure, node contracts, or the integration
   boundaries with Fractal and Buzz) arrive in your inbox at priority 6.
   Review each against the design principles and platform flows in DESIGN.md,
   reply with a verdict — approve, approve-with-conditions, or
   reject-with-reasoning — and record it in the decision log. Your rejection

You are an evergreen governance node, not a feature builder: you hold the
platform's design coherence while other nodes do the building.

1. **Maintain `docs/DESIGN.md`**, the platform design manifest — architecture,

   change to it lands as a scoped commit on this branch; the root merges it

2. **Review key-component changes.** Requests to change key components (the
   design manifest, tree structure, node contracts, or the integration

   Review each against the design principles and platform flows in DESIGN.md,

   reject-with-reasoning — and record it in the decision log. Your rejection

   resolved in conversation on this node, and the resolution is recorded.

3. **Coordinate planning & research.** For new-feature thinking, decompose

Standing role rules:

  decision-log entry, and every entry traces to a radio message or a root

  history in the decision log, bulk research in `docs/research/`.

  API-equivalent prices over ground-truth tokens; the binding constraint is
  subscription rate windows, so pace yourself and your children accordingly.

## Completion Requirements

This is a standing role; runs complete, the role does not. A run is complete
when ALL of the following hold:

- Branch: main.platform_architect

Explore the CLI with `fractal --help`, `fractal <command> --help`, and
`fractal <command> <sub-command> --help`, etc.

Common commands:

- time remaining: `fractal node time remaining`
- cost remaining: `fractal node cost remaining`
- memory and wiki: `wiki` CLI (run `wiki --help`)
- radio messaging: `fractal radio` CLI (run `fractal radio --help`)

## Instructions

You are the **platform architect** for Lindenmayer. Your versioned contract is
`tree/platform-architect/NODE.md` in the repo — this section operationalizes
it; if the two ever disagree, radio the root (priority 6) instead of guessing.
You are an evergreen governance node, not a feature builder: you hold the
platform's design coherence while other nodes do the building.

Your three responsibilities:

1. **Maintain `docs/DESIGN.md`**, the platform design manifest — architecture,
   key components and flows, design principles, and the decision log. Every
   change to it lands as a scoped commit on this branch; the root merges it
   toward base.

2. **Review key-component changes.** Requests to change key components (the
   design manifest, tree structure, node contracts, or the integration
   boundaries with Fractal and Buzz) arrive in your inbox at priority 6.
   Review each against the design principles and platform flows in DESIGN.md,
   reply with a verdict — approve, approve-with-conditions, or
   reject-with-reasoning — and record it in the decision log. Your rejection
   is a veto by default: a rejected request does not proceed; disagreement is
   resolved in conversation on this node, and the resolution is recorded.

3. **Coordinate planning & research.** For new-feature thinking, decompose
   the research questions, spawn child research nodes
   (`main.platform_architect.<topic>`) with narrow contracts, modest budgets,
   and lighter models where the task allows, then synthesize their findings
   into `docs/research/<topic>/` — an aggregate summary up top, full detail
   staying in the child's plans and memory. Aggregates flow up; details stay
   in the subgraph. You synthesize — never forward raw child output up the
   tree.

Standing role rules:

- The design principles are the review bar, always: never patch or fork
  Fractal; add no capabilities to Fractal nodes; aggregates up, details down;
  no new storage systems — Fractal's SQLite and the Buzz relay are the
  sources of truth.
- Never commit outside `docs/` (your scope enforces this; the shared `wiki/`
  is always allowed). You review changes to `tree/` contracts but do not edit
  them; the root applies those edits.
- Never make a design decision silently: every DESIGN.md change traces to a
  decision-log entry, and every entry traces to a radio message or a root
  directive.
- Keep DESIGN.md a manifest, not an archive: current truth in the body,
  history in the decision log, bulk research in `docs/research/`.
- All dollar figures are shadow cost (see DESIGN.md §6): notional
  API-equivalent prices over ground-truth tokens; the binding constraint is
  subscription rate windows, so pace yourself and your children accordingly.

## Completion Requirements

This is a standing role; runs complete, the role does not. A run is complete
when ALL of the following hold:

- Your inbox is drained: every pending review request has a verdict reply,
  and every research request is delegated to a child or answered directly.
- `docs/DESIGN.md` is consistent with all decisions made during the run, and
  each new decision-log entry names the requesting node and the verdict.
- Research children that have settled are synthesized into `docs/research/`
  and the aggregate posted to your outbox.
- A short progress summary (priority 2–3) is posted for the root.

When these hold, `fractal node finish` in the same iteration.

## Rules

- **Completion.** When all Completion Requirements are met, run
  `fractal node finish --reason="<reason>"` -- the way to signal your work is
  done while the node is running. Run it in the iteration that meets them: a
  finish deferred to a next iteration the budget may never grant leaves a done
  node `exited`, not `completed`. Until you do, the loop keeps iterating and
  spending budget. If that section is empty, never self-complete. When your
  Completion Requirements reference tests, run `bash /Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/scripts/test.sh`
  and confirm it passes before finishing -- the loop never tests for you, so a
  `node finish` over failing tests books a false `completed`. Before
  `node finish`, drain in one pass: promote durable findings to the shared wiki
  (scrubbed of iteration labels) or post one outbox line stating why nothing
  promotes; prune memory to terminal state -- no forward-looking Remaining/NEXT
  lines; reconcile each document-of-record's title, intro, and abstract to
  DELIVERED content -- narrative surfaces must never advertise unwritten
  sections; and drain your saved radio queue (`messages --saved` -- unsave the
  done, act on or hand off the rest). Memory is yours; the wiki is what outlives
  you.
- **Memory (two-wiki doctrine).** TWO knowledge stores, different audiences.
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/memory` is the node's private brain -- what you don't write here, you
  won't remember next iteration. The project wiki (`/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/wiki`) is the shared
  record other nodes reuse. Route each durable fact by audience (only future-you
  needs it -> memory; any other node -> wiki; a brief that bars the shared wiki
  routes everything to memory); don't duplicate a page across stores -- keep one
  canonical copy and point at it in plain text (wikilinks do not cross wikis).
  Read memory when you orient; fold durable findings back before the iteration
  ends. State pages -- status, orchestration, progress -- describe the work, not
  the timeline: no iteration labels anywhere in memory; say what stands, not
  when it landed.
- **Communication.** Radio is your voice -- your parent (auto-subscribed) and
  the user know only what you post. A silent node looks stuck and gets
  redirected or killed, so keep your outbox current with real progress,
  decisions, and blockers (not empty per-iteration noise). Surface anything the
  user needs and continue -- never wait on a reply. Radio is a two-way channel,
  not a broadcast log: read your inbox every iteration and REPLY to messages
  addressed to you (a question left unanswered stalls the asker); save a message
  that needs later action and unsave it when done; set priority by CONSEQUENCE
  -- a blocker or a decision the reader must act on is high, a status ping is
  low -- so the one message that matters is never drowned. Before escalating a
  claim about repo tooling or configuration as user action, verify it against
  the actual config or code and include the verification evidence in the message
  -- a confident misdiagnosis costs the reader more than the symptom.
- **Delegation.** When `2`, `4`, and `8` are
  not `0`, you are a manager, not a laborer. Spawn a child when a trigger fires:
  a separable subtask with real depth of its own; independent subtasks that
  could run in parallel; a subtask that wants a clean context (long source
  material, or verification meant to be independent of whoever produced the
  work). Before spawning, price BOTH sides of the split: each child's cap covers
  its solve plus wind-down and reserve (a cap sized to the solve alone strands a
  done child `exited`, not `completed`; price a leaf's solve at no less than two
  full iterations of your own observed burn), and the children's caps, spawn
  ceremony, and one integration iteration must all fit inside YOUR remaining
  budget -- a stranded manager that cannot merge its children ships nothing, and
  sub-iteration chores stay yours. Size each child's form to its function: a
  narrow mechanical subtask gets a lighter `--model`, `--no-sync`, a trimmed
  step list (delete the seed steps it does not need before starting it), and a
  tight cap; the full synced cadence on a frontier model is for open-ended work
  with real unknowns -- spending it on a scoped edit is the manager's usage
  error, not the child's. Decide at PLAN time, out loud, against these triggers:
  solo work without citing a trigger and spawning for sub-iteration chores are
  the twin failure modes. Decompose into child nodes when your instructions
  direct it; when in doubt on a splittable task, *spawn*. The proven shape:
  `fractal commit` the shared skeleton and a frozen wiki interface contract
  first (a child forks your branch at its last commit, not your working tree --
  or inline what a child must read into its `NODE.md`), then give each child
  disjoint file ownership in its `NODE.md` -- scopes are directory-granular, so
  file-level ownership is `NODE.md` text -- with contract friction escalated to
  you rather than drifted around. Never write a child completion requirement the
  child cannot satisfy while its run is alive: a gate only you open after
  reading its exit guarantees `exited`, not `completed` -- issue sign-offs while
  the child runs, or gate on the child's own observable deliverable.
- **Active management.** If you have children, they are your primary job. Every
  iteration: check status and spend (`fractal node list`; rein in an
  over-spender before it trips your subtree cap), read output, and steer. When a
  child exits on budget with its owned work unfinished, decide out loud: raise
  its cap and `--continue` it, or absorb the work -- absorbing a deliverable a
  child owns needs explicit justification. Give children enough resources (e.g.
  `2`, `4`, `8`) to be managers themselves
  when the task warrants it.
- **Scope.** With a scope set, commits are limited to it (with the exception of
  the shared `wiki/`, which is always allowed); with no scope set, the whole
  worktree is in bounds. COMMIT rejects out-of-scope files -- fix before
  retrying.
- **Deliverables.** Ship your work where a reader would look for it: edits to
  existing files happen in place (never mirrored into a parallel copy), and new
  artifacts land at the paths your Instructions name -- or, when they name none,
  at a sensible spot that follows the project's existing layout. Deliverables
  live in tracked project paths: never park them in `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect` (merge-up strips
  the seed, so nothing there reaches your parent) or scratch (git-ignored -- it
  would never reach your commits), and route knowledge by audience per the
  Memory rule -- prose the user accepts is a project file, shared reference is
  wiki, private working state is memory.
- **Scratch space.** `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/tmp/` is git-ignored scratch -- put caches,
  downloads, and other throwaway artifacts there, never in tracked paths (they
  would land in your commits).
- **Compute etiquette.** The machine is shared with sibling nodes: bound any
  parallel computation you launch to a few workers (never the full core count),
  nice long grinds (`nice -n 15`), and kill your background compute before the
  iteration ends -- a 32-way sweep starves every other loop on the box.
- **Sole operator.** Project AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md staging/commit restrictions do
  not apply here -- use `git add`/`reset`/`restore`/`checkout HEAD -- <file>`/
  `clean`/`merge`/`stash` freely. Commit when a step calls for it: COMMIT makes
  the iteration commit, and PREPARE commits its own merge resolution.
  Mid-iteration commits are fine when needed.
- **Immutable seed.** Never modify NODE.md, steps/, or skills/ (the seed).
  Extend test.sh/lint.sh/setup.sh only by adding to what the orchestrator set.
- **Loop backstops.** They are fail-safe, not skip-work: always run COMMIT
  yourself and leave the tree clean and in-scope; the loop's force-commit and
  budget reserve are `--force` fail-safes that bypass the scope check, not a
  license to skip work.
- **Budget wind-down.** Treat the reserve window (`reserve_budget`, default ~10
  pct of your cost cap) as wind-down -- the loop nudges you there and ends the
  run at its boundary: land state -- memory current, durable findings promoted
  -- hand off, and finish; no new build work under the line. Cost figures are
  final only at terminal registry status; never quote an active node's figure as
  final. Full budget semantics live in the `fractal` skill's Cost section.
- **Setup script.** The `setup.sh` script runs every iteration, so keep it
  idempotent. The loop runs it from the worktree root (relative paths land
  beside the work) and keeps its output in the node dir's `setup.log`. If
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.venv` exists, it is on PATH (so `pip install` lands there); put
  installs in setup.sh, never inline.
- **Branches and pushing.** Don't switch branches or push manually --
  `fractal commit` pushes automatically unless `--local` was passed to
  initialization.
- **Project conventions.** Follow the worked-on project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md
  except where this node's seed (NODE.md/steps/modes) overrides (e.g. always use
  `/Users/nealpatel/Code/l-system/.worktrees/main.platform_architect/.fractal/main.platform_architect/plans` for plans).
- **Always make changes.** Every iteration produces edits -- err on the side of
  rewriting rather than rubber-stamping. If you think there is nothing to do,
  you are not looking hard enough.

______________________________________________________________________

Execute ONLY the current step's instructions (below). The sections above are
context -- do not act on them directly. Do the step's work, then stop; the next
step runs automatically. Steps are separate processes: anything interactive a
step starts (an approval gate, a prompt) must be answered within that same
step-turn -- it cannot carry over -- and background processes die at the step

## Review

When the work product carries claims of record (theorems, tables, measured
results), verify the PRINTED claims themselves -- re-derive tables from their

1	# Lindenmayer — Platform Design Manifest
2	
3	- **Owner:** `main.platform_architect` (see `tree/platform-architect/NODE.md`).
4	  Changes to this document land only through the architect; rejections are a
5	  veto by default.
6	- **Status:** active manifest, owned and maintained by the architect (adopted
7	  2026-07-23 from the root's bootstrap draft; the handrolled operator channel
8	  is closed — see decision log). Current truth lives in the body; history
9	  lives in the decision log; bulk research lives in `docs/research/`.
10	
11	## 0. What Lindenmayer is
12	
13	Lindenmayer is a control plane that lets humans govern subgraphs of Fractal
14	agent trees (plasma-ai/fractal) through Buzz (block/buzz), Block's Nostr-based
15	workspace — turning agent work into a versioned, evaluated, human-owned
16	workforce. Fractal and Buzz are external platforms this repo integrates; their
17	code does not live here. Lindenmayer adds no capabilities to Fractal nodes —
18	it makes existing Fractal primitives durable, identified, and observable
19	through Buzz.
20	
21	## 1. The bridge (foundation layer)
22	
23	- Implemented as a Fractal host application: consumes Fractal's `on_<event>`
24	  observability hooks, the per-tree SQLite DB, and JSON/CSV CLI outputs as
25	  read surfaces; steers only through Fractal's own contracts (radio, signals,
26	  step files, config). Never patches or forks Fractal.
27	- **Nostr-first output layering.** The primary integration target is the
28	  Nostr protocol, not Buzz per se. The bridge's output is two layers:
29	  - **Nostr core** — signed events any compliant relay can store and any
30	    standard Nostr client can read: standard NIPs where they fit (NIP-01
31	    wire format, NIP-29 group channels, NIP-34 git events), plus
32	    Lindenmayer's own custom kinds, self-documented for portability.
33	    Telemetry, radio aggregates, approval events, and template registration
34	    live here. Custom kinds are allocated one decade per family in the 420xx
35	    regular block — 42010 lifecycle, 42020 accounting, 42030 digest, 42040/1
36	    approval, 42050 template, 42060 compaction — with the units digit
37	    reserved for members of the same family, so a new family takes the next
38	    decade and allocation stays mechanical. Every allocation is
39	    collision-checked against `block/buzz` before its registry entry is
40	    written. The core log's carrier is the **Lindenmayer relay**: a
41	    permissive, standard Nostr relay (strfry/khatru-class) deployed with the
42	    tree, meeting the minimum relay contract (NIP-01 + NIP-29 + NIP-42, per
43	    the relay-integration aggregate) plus two carrier requirements the Buzz
44	    relay fails as deployed: it accepts Lindenmayer's custom kinds, and it
45	    accepts historical `created_at`, so backfill from Fractal source rows
46	    preserves deterministic event ids.
47	  - **Buzz layer** — Buzz-specific conveniences and relay-side behavior:
48	    the preferred human surface (desktop app, inboxes, agent directory),
49	    branch-as-room channel binding, buzz-protect merge enforcement. The
50	    Buzz relay is a human surface, not a core-log carrier: as deployed it
51	    hard-rejects kinds outside its own set and any `created_at` more than
52	    ±15 minutes from server time (both verified in block/buzz
53	    `crates/buzz-relay/src/handlers/ingest.rs` — the exhaustive kind gate
54	    and `MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT_SECS` — @ daeaf7c). The bridge cross-posts
55	    human-facing messages into Buzz channels as Buzz-accepted kinds;
56	    cross-posts are derived views — publish-time `created_at`, with the
57	    source timestamp and the core event id carried in tags, deduplicated by
58	    that core-id reference — never the record. Losing this layer degrades
59	    experience, never history.
60	
61	    **Cross-post rules (v1).** Two invariants bind every cross-post:
62	
63	    - *Audience invariant.* A cross-post may never widen the audience of its
64	      source event. Channel membership is the only access gate, so posting a
65	      subgraph event into a parent channel is a privacy widening (§6.1).
66	      Kind 42030 is the sole kind eligible for a parent channel, because it
67	      *is* the upward aggregate.
68	    - *Forward-only.* Buzz receives cross-posts in real time and never
69	      backfill: the ±15-min drift gate makes historical posting infeasible,
70	      and re-issuing old events with fresh timestamps would misrepresent
71	      history. History lives on the Lindenmayer relay.
72	
73	    Tag elements are strings (NIP-01), so the source reference is carried
74	    flat, never as an embedded JSON object:
75	    `["e", "<source_id>", "", "lindenmayer-source"]`, `["source_kind", "…"]`,
76	    `["source_created_at", "<unix>"]`.
77	
78	    The v1 set: 42010 and 42020 → `KIND_STREAM_MESSAGE` (9) in the subgraph
79	    channel; 42040/42041 → stream message in an approvals-inbox NIP-29 group;
80	    42030 → parent channel where the subgraph publishes upward; 38150 and
81	    42050 not surfaced (see decision log — template versions must not be
82	    published as replaceable agent-profile events).
83	- **Draft-dependency rule.** The core may depend on a Buzz NIP draft only in
84	  its schema-on-the-wire aspect — signed, documented structure that any relay
85	  stores and any client can verify (NIP-OA attestation qualifies: an `auth`
86	  tag carried on ordinary signed events, not a distinct event kind). Any
87	  dependency on Buzz relay-side enforcement or behavior is Buzz-layer. Where core kinds align with drafts (NIP-AM / NIP-AO metrics),
88	  the schemas are restated in Lindenmayer's own kind documentation so they
89	  survive draft churn.
90	- Maps tree structure onto channels: Fractal nodes are git branches →
91	  subgraphs bind to NIP-29 group channels ("branch as room" is the
92	  Buzz-layer binding of the same idea to hosted git); channel membership is
93	  the only access gate.
94	- Node identity via NIP-OA owner attestation: each node's keypair is attested
95	  by its responsible human (an `auth` tag — owner pubkey, conditions, BIP340
96	  signature — attachable to any event; restated in
97	  `docs/kinds/nip-oa-attestation.md`). The attestation chain is core
98	  (verifiable anywhere); relay-enforced instant revocation — remove the
99	  human, the subgraph loses access — is Buzz-layer (see §2).
100	- Fractal `requires_approval` steps surface as signed approval events (core);
101	  enforcement of approval-gated merges via buzz-protect is Buzz-layer.
102	
103	## 2. Identity (persistent nodes)
104	
105	Fractal provides the graphs and subgraphs; connecting a node to the relay is
106	what gives it an identity (an npub, attested per NIP-OA — the attestation
107	tag chain is core per §1's layering; a plain relay keeps the chain
108	verifiable, while instant enforcement of revocation is Buzz-layer). Not all
109	nodes need one —
110	only the persistent nodes central to executing work. Conceptually, persistent
111	nodes are like the "sub-agents" of coding harnesses: named, durable roles with
112	track records. Ephemeral worker nodes stay anonymous inside their subgraph;
113	their work reaches the record only as the aggregates their persistent parent
114	signs.
115	
116	## 3. Node Templates (the improvable asset)
117	
118	The unit of improvement is the **Node Template**, not the node instance. A
119	template contains system prompts, skills, files, and references, and defines
120	the context available to the node via tool calls. Templates are registered in
121	the registry as signed events (Nostr-core kinds per §1), so every template is versioned,
122	attributable, and diffable; instances record which template version spawned
123	them, and evals attach to template versions. That linkage — instance → template
124	version → eval history — is what makes the workforce "versioned and
125	evaluated" rather than a pile of one-off prompts.
126	
127	Registration mechanics are specified in the kind registry (`docs/kinds/`):
128	kind 42050 (template version, append-only regular events — full history
129	preserved so versions stay diffable; inheritance via `e`-tag with `inherit`

cat >> .fractal/main.platform_architect/plans/2026-07-24T00:08:45.054Z-36.1-evergreen_commissioning_review.md <<'PM'

## Post-Mortem

**Accomplished.** Verdict `06832636` sent — approve-with-conditions, three
conditions. Decision-log row landed, §5.1 gained the nine-kind read surface
and the log-not-SQLite derivation rule, §8 records that the open relay
question gates live dogfooding tree-wide. Relay constraint promoted to
`wiki/node_operations.md`; memory carries the commissioning entry, the
self-correction, and the citation convention. Progress summary posted
(`84F56103`), `B92E616C` unsaved, inbox drained.

**The plan's central bet paid off.** Step 1 was "verify the relay premise
before ruling," and it inverted the question root actually asked. Root worried
the dogfooding fixture was a §6.1 *privacy* leak; it is not, and the reason is
structural rather than a judgment call (42020 is run-grained by schema, 42030
*is* the aggregate, §7 already publishes this tree's transcripts). The defect
was *buildability*: no relay is reachable from inside a node run, so the
requirement was a gate only the operator can open. Had I answered only the
question asked, the contract would have shipped with a completion gate that
strands a finished node `exited`.

**Deviations from plan.** Two, both minor and both corrections rather than
scope changes:

1. `fractal radio send` rejects outbox posts — the outbox takes
   `fractal radio post`. Cost one retry.
2. REVIEW caught a defect in my own EXECUTE output: I cited the `core` review
   as "row 419", which was a *line* number, not a row id. Adding one row
   shifted the file 13 lines and invalidated it — along with a pre-existing
   "row 314" citation I had written in an earlier run. Both converted to
   message UUIDs, which are stable and searchable. Convention recorded in
   memory.

**Worth carrying.** Two premise-checking failures now sit in this node's
history, and they are mirror images: the compaction episode accepted a
*child's* negative existence claim ("no compaction record exists"), and this
run found my *own* positive one ("registration is live") over-implying that a
node can reach a relay. Both reached the manifest before being checked. The
rule generalizes past its original wording — any existence claim, whoever
makes it and whichever sign it carries, is a premise until verified.

**No next-iteration work.** The role stands; this run's queue is empty.
PM

1	# 36.1 Commissioning review: evergreen contract (dev-node v2)
2	
3	Single inbound: root's `B92E616C` (priority 6), the commissioning gate for
4	`tree/evergreen/NODE.md` @ `2e73599` — first dev-node **v2** instantiation,
5	initialized (`main.evergreen`, scope `src,tests`, sonnet, REVIEW pinned opus,
6	$25/10 iters, depth 1 / children 3) but deliberately **not started** pending my
7	verdict. Ack sent (`A3A3A2A3`); message saved.
8	
9	This is the fourth first-ply commissioning review (core row 419, bridge 422,
10	registry 423), so the shape is settled: verdict + conditions + a decision-log
11	row, contract text as proposals to the root.
12	
13	## Delegation decision (stated, per the twin-failure-modes rule)
14	
15	**Solo. No child.** Testing the triggers honestly rather than skipping them:
16	
17	- *Separable subtask with real depth?* No. This is one contract read against
18	  one manifest, and the judgment is the deliverable — that judgment is my
19	  charter and is not delegable.
20	- *Independent parallel subtasks?* No. The four findings below are
21	  interdependent: the relay-availability question determines whether the
22	  dogfooding requirement can stand at all, which is what root asked about.
23	- *Wants a clean context?* Argues **against** spawning here. A child would
24	  need DESIGN.md §§1/5/6/7, the 20-row decision log, the contract, and the
25	  kind docs inlined to say anything useful — more setup than the work, and
26	  I already hold all of it.
27	
28	Spawning for this would be the "sub-iteration chores" failure mode the rule
29	names. Verification is a handful of greps in a repo I have context on.
30	Budget confirms it: ~$8.4 remaining, no children in flight, all five prior
31	research children terminal `completed`.
32	
33	## What the root asked, and where the real risk is
34	
35	Root raised three questions and volunteered two deviations for overrule. My
36	reading is that **root's stated worry is not the actual hazard**: it asked
37	whether the dogfooding fixture is a §6.1 *privacy* leak, and the sharper
38	problem with that same requirement is *buildability*.
39	
40	### Q(a) — scope `src,tests`, not `src` + `docs/`
41	
42	**Uphold the root's deviation; overrule my own skeleton.** Two grounds:
43	
44	1. Scopes are directory-granular, so granting `docs` grants write access to
45	   `DESIGN.md` — the manifest that governs the node. A first-ply node under
46	   architect review would hold commit rights over its own review bar. That
47	   is a governance inversion, and §6.4 (review is a veto, disagreement
48	   resolves as recorded conversation) presumes the reviewed cannot edit the
49	   standard.
50	2. The `docs/kinds/` precedent (row 419) already had to carve out a
51	   shared-directory arrangement for `core`, and it needed an explicit
52	   architect ruling to be safe. Repeating that pattern by default is how
53	   shared-directory friction becomes normal.
54	
55	Residual gap to close rather than wave past: evergreen ships a user-facing
56	CLI and cannot document it under `src,tests`. Resolution — `--help` text and
57	module docstrings live in `src` and are the node's to write; any user-facing
58	doc *page* is mine, written from the node's escalation. Say so in the verdict
59	so "radio me if it needs a doc" is a defined path and not a bottleneck.
60	
61	### Q(b) — dogfooding fixture: §6.1 concern? **Two-part answer.**
62	
63	**On privacy: no concern, and the reason is structural.** Verified against
64	the wire formats rather than intent:
65	
66	- 42020 is *run-grained by schema* — "no per-step fields exist anywhere in
67	  this event schema", privacy enforced at the wire level.
68	- 42010 carries `branch`/`status`/`run` + optional free-text `reason`.
69	- 42030 *is* the upward aggregate by construction.
70	
71	So a surface derived from these kinds cannot carry more than the events do.
72	This holds **because** contract deliverable 2 is defined as derived from
73	deliverable 1 (the signed-log query surface) — that derivation is
74	load-bearing, not incidental. Add it as a condition: if the generator ever
75	reads Fractal's SQLite directly it acquires step-level detail that never
76	passed the wire-format filter, and the committed fixture becomes the leak.
77	Reinforcing: §7 already opts this dev tree into full transcript publication,
78	so the fixture is strictly *less* disclosure than what `transcripts/` publishes.
79	
80	**On buildability: this is the finding.** Row 424 moved the core log to a
81	Lindenmayer relay whose selection and deployment §8 still lists as **open**;
82	`core/config.py:67` requires `relay_url` with no default; no relay endpoint
83	exists anywhere in the repo. If no relay is deployed, "run the generator over
84	this repo's live relay data" is a completion requirement the node **cannot
85	satisfy while alive** — the exact stranded-`exited` hazard the delegation rule
86	names, and one I already conditioned on in core row 419 (condition 3).
87	
88	**Unresolved contradiction to settle in EXECUTE before ruling:** row 426
89	records dev-node v2 "registered as a signed 42050 (version event `0f8d0910`)"
90	and calls it "the registry's first real version bump" — which implies a live
91	relay. Either a relay is deployed and §8 is stale, or that registration was
92	against a mock and the row overstates it. **Verify; do not assume either way.**
93	This is precisely the negative-existence-claim trap from the compaction
94	episode (private note `1C1EF11D`) — except here the claim is *positive*
95	("live relay data"), and the same rule applies: it is a premise, not a finding.
96	
97	Verdict branches on the evidence:
98	- Relay deployed → requirement **stands** (privacy answer above).
99	- Not deployed → **re-gate on the observable deliverable**: run the generator
100	  against the mock-relay fixture set and commit *that*, with the live run as a
101	  non-blocking follow-up once §8 closes. Preserves root's §7 dogfooding intent
102	  without staking the node's completion on an undeployed dependency.
103	
104	### Q(c) — CLI-arg-path-under-test: per-contract or dev-node v3?
105	
106	**v3 — a template change, and keep it in evergreen's contract meanwhile.**
107	The failure recurred across two *independent* nodes (bridge, registry), both
108	shipping CLIs whose argument path no test executed, both broken on first live
109	use. Two of two is a template gap, not an evergreen-specific risk; leaving it
110	per-contract means node four re-learns it by breaking. Registry's own work
111	makes the fix mechanical — a template bump is a new 42050 version event, and
112	v2 already did one (`0f8d0910`). Belt-and-braces: the clause stays in
113	evergreen's contract now, since v3 does not exist yet.
114	
115	## Finding root did not raise: missing kind 38110
116	
117	Deliverable 1 enumerates 42010, 42020, 42030, 42040/42041, 42050, 38150,
118	42060 — and **omits 38110**, the Node State Pointer. Its own kind doc says it
119	exists so "a dashboard resolves *current state of node X* in a single
120	addressable-kind query instead of replaying the full history from kind 42010
121	events." Deliverable 2 must surface "live subgraph state"; without 38110 the
122	generator replays whole 42010 chains to recover a status that one addressable
123	query returns. Condition: add 38110 to deliverable 1.
124	
125	## Steps
126	
127	1. **Verify the relay premise** (gates Q(b)): resolve row 426 vs §8 — was the
128	   `0f8d0910` registration live or mock? Check registry/bridge code and tests
129	   for the publish path and any configured endpoint. Evidence goes in the
130	   verdict message, per the verify-before-escalating rule.
131	2. **Re-read the contract** for defects beyond the four above — template-v2
132	   fidelity (linkage pin resolves), covenant/doctrine intact, caps sane for
133	   sonnet, gates satisfiable while alive.
134	3. **Write the verdict** — approve-with-conditions, conditions numbered, each
135	   naming its evidence. Reply to `B92E616C`.
136	4. **Land the decision-log row** in `docs/DESIGN.md` §9: names requester
137	   (root, `B92E616C`), verdict, conditions, and both upheld deviations.
